old_claim.note: Basic Woodworking is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: The securely sourced early bellows evidence is a ceramic pot-bellows and tuyere-nozzle metalworking assemblage, so pottery provides the material path for this first-known bellows form.

source_shape.source_locator: Area A Stratum A2 table and Iron Age I remains narrative identify pot-bellows and tuyere nozzles among the metal-working remains.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The excavation report anchors the bellows evidence to an Iron IB metalworking context where the preserved bellows form is ceramic pot bellows with tuyere nozzles.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because the corrected node is dated from the preserved pot-bellows assemblage, pottery is the source-backed material path, while the previous woodworking edge only cited unrelated Pleistocene wooden artifacts.

ontology_before: Bellows depended on woodworking_basic through a source that documented ancient wooden artifacts but did not document bellows or metalworking forced draft.
ontology_after: Bellows is anchored to an Iron Age metalworking pot-bellows assemblage, with pottery as the sourced enabling material path for the first-known evidence.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: bellows no longer directly depends on woodworking_basic and now depends on pottery as an enabling edge.
would_reject_if: A source-backed earlier non-ceramic bellows assemblage is added and the node is split away from the pot-bellows chronology.
The node is narrowed to carved wooden bellows rather than the current forced-draft metalworking scope.
A primary source shows the Abel Beth Maacah pot-bellows interpretation is incorrect or not connected to bellows use.
short_reason: The source-backed first-known bellows evidence is a pot-bellows metalworking assemblage, not unrelated woodworking.
